text.title: Design Phases and NFR Quantification
text.summary: Five-phase system design flow, functional vs non-functional requirement definitions, NFR attribute table with measurable targets, and back-of-envelope capacity formulas.

rule.statement: Execute design in five phases in order: Understand (requirements list), Scope (in/out of scope), Design (architecture + diagrams), Validate (adversarial review), Document (ADRs + C4). The Validate phase must be a separate agent call — never a closing paragraph of the Design phase.
rule.rationale: Agents that skip the Validate phase produce designs without identified single points of failure, hot partitions, or data-loss windows. Adversarial review requires a different prompt persona, not an afterthought.

list.item: Understand: user stories with acceptance criteria, use cases with actors and flows, API contracts, integration points, clarifying questions.
Scope: explicitly list what is in scope AND what is out of scope. Out-of-scope items prevent scope creep in design.
Design: architecture diagrams (C4 L1+L2 minimum), data model, API contracts, pattern selection with trade-off matrix.

Validate: stress-test the design — single points of failure, hot partitions, data-loss windows, fan-out amplifiers, silent-failure modes. Reject mitigations that are "add monitoring" without a concrete metric and threshold.
Document: ADR per non-obvious decision, C4 diagrams in code (Mermaid/Structurizr), spec.md with NFRs and scale estimates.

rule.statement: Force NFR numerics before any diagram: an LLM with vague NFRs will always over-engineer. Every NFR must have a measurable target and a source (user-impact analysis or competitive benchmark).
rule.rationale: Vague NFRs ("it should be fast") produce architectures sized for 100x the actual load because the LLM defaults to the largest example in its training data.

list.item: Scalability: DAU, RPS at peak, concurrent users. Typical formula: Peak_RPS = (DAU * requests_per_user / 86400) * 3.
Availability: 99.9% = 8.76h downtime/year, 99.99% = 52min/year. Source from business SLA, not aspiration.
Latency: p50, p95, p99 in milliseconds. Typical targets: p50 < 100ms, p95 < 200ms, p99 < 500ms for web APIs.
Durability: RPO (max data loss window), RTO (max recovery time). Source from business impact analysis.
Consistency: strong vs eventual (CAP: CP vs AP). Driven by the data's business invariant, not convenience.
Security: threat model completed, compliance scope identified (GDPR, SOC2, PCI). Not optional.

rule.statement: Write RPS/storage/bandwidth calculations in the design prompt; do not let the agent skip to architecture without these numbers. LLM scale estimates without anchors are off by an order of magnitude.
rule.rationale: Architecture choices are load-sensitive: a service at 10 RPS can be a single-instance monolith; at 100k RPS it needs sharding. Getting this wrong at design time is expensive to reverse.

example.code:
# Traffic DAU = 100,000 RPS = DAU * 10 requests/user / 86400 = ~11.6 RPS Peak_RPS = 11.6 * 3 = ~35 RPS
# Storage per year objects_per_day = DAU * 2 = 200,000 avg_object_size = 1 KB storage_per_day = 200,000 * 1 KB = ~200 MB storage_per_year = 200 MB * 365 * 3 (replication) = ~219 GB
# Cache cache_size = 0.20 * total_data # 20% hot data rule of thumb
example.note: Use these numbers as inputs to the architecture prompt before generating any diagram.

list.item: All NFRs have measurable numeric targets.
Data model supports all use cases.
Migration path defined if replacing an existing system.
